student_name: Eliane Portela Nogueira Lima de Brito
hub_subtitle: Business English for a doctor rebuilding her professional voice

lesson.menu_title: In the Room Where It Happens — Running and Participating in Business Meetings
lesson.menu_desc: Indirect and polite questions for running and joining business meetings entirely in English -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- In the Room Where It Happens
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eliane Portela | Aula 6 -- In the Room Where It Happens

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to lead a meeting and help the discussion run smoothly
the written record of what was decided in a meeting
general agreement among a group of people
to bring people to the same understanding or goal
to give your opinion on a topic under discussion
to postpone a topic to a later meeting
to return to a topic later
to make brief contact to share a quick update
involving people from several different departments
a small group that splits off from the main meeting
in agreement, sharing the same understanding

reading.rtitle: In the Room Where It Happens
paras: In many companies, the person who runs a meeting well is not the loudest voice in the room; it is the one who can facilitate. To facilitate a meeting means to guide the discussion, keep it on time, and make sure every voice is heard. A good facilitator opens with a clear agenda, invites quieter colleagues to weigh in, and takes minutes so that the decisions are not forgotten the moment the call ends.

Modern projects are almost always cross-functional, which means that people from different departments -- finance, operations, technology, and the clinical team -- sit at the same table. Getting these groups aligned is hard, because each one arrives with its own priorities. When a topic is not ready for a decision, an experienced leader will table it and circle back once the right people have had time to touch base offline. Sometimes the group splits into a breakout session, so a smaller team can dig into the details and then report back to everyone.

The goal of any meeting is a real consensus, not a silence that only looks like agreement. Before they adjourn, strong facilitators check that everyone is genuinely on the same page: they summarize the decisions, confirm who owns each next step, and ask whether anything is still unclear. It is far better to spend two extra minutes making sure the room agrees than to discover a week later that half the team understood something completely different.
reading.source: Adapted from a professional-communication guide, The Executive Voice

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the article?
choices: A good meeting is one where the loudest and most senior person makes every decision.
Running a meeting well means facilitating: guiding the discussion, aligning people, and confirming a real consensus before you finish.
Cross-functional projects should be avoided because different departments never agree.

items: A good facilitator keeps the meeting on time and makes sure quieter colleagues weigh in.
The text says a good facilitator keeps it on time and invites quieter colleagues to weigh in.
The article says the loudest voice in the room usually runs the best meetings.
The text says the person who runs a meeting well is not the loudest voice; it is the one who can facilitate.
When a topic is not ready for a decision, an experienced leader will table it and circle back later.
The text says the leader will table it and circle back once people have had time to touch base.
A real consensus is the same as a silence that looks like agreement.
The text says the goal is a real consensus, not a silence that only looks like agreement.

gapfill.text: Notice: verbs like facilitate, table, and circle back describe HOW a meeting is run. Nouns like consensus and minutes describe the RESULT. Knowing which is which helps you use them accurately.

bank.label: Useful meeting language
items: Could you tell me what is on the agenda?
Would you like to weigh in on this?
Let's table that and circle back next week.
I'll take the minutes and send them after the call.
Are we all on the same page?
Let's adjourn and touch base tomorrow.

lf.title: Which polite opener, and how formal?
items: if you could weigh in on this.
explaining who will facilitate the breakout?

lf.text: Notice: after the polite opener, the sentence keeps normal word order (subject + verb) and drops the auxiliary 'do'. 'Where is the report?' becomes 'Could you tell me where the report is?' -- not 'where is the report'.

items.situation: You join a meeting late and need to know what has already been decided. Ask the facilitator politely.
tips: Could you tell me what we have decided so far?
Do you know whether the team reached a consensus?

items.situation: You are not sure who is leading the training breakout. Ask a colleague in a polite, indirect way.
tips: Would you mind telling me who will facilitate the breakout session?
Do you know who is running the breakout?

items.situation: You want a quiet colleague from the finance team to give an opinion on the budget.
tips: I was wondering if you could weigh in on the budget.
Would you like to weigh in here?

items.situation: The meeting is running long and you need to check the finish time before you commit to another call.
tips: Could you tell me when we plan to adjourn?
Do you know how much longer the meeting will run?

items: You are facilitating a cross-functional kickoff for your clinic project. Open the meeting: welcome everyone, state the agenda, and invite people to weigh in.
You need three pieces of information from the technology partner: the timeline, who owns the training, and whether the budget is approved. Ask all three as polite, indirect questions.
A topic is not ready for a decision. Politely table it, explain that you will circle back next week, and suggest a breakout session for the details.
Close the meeting: confirm the consensus, check that everyone is on the same page, say you will send the minutes, and adjourn.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model language
list: Open a meeting: 'Good morning, everyone. Let me facilitate today. First on the agenda is...'
Ask politely / indirectly: 'Could you tell me how the process works?' / 'Do you know whether the budget is approved?' / 'I was wondering if you could weigh in.'
Manage the room: 'Let's table that point and circle back next week.' / 'Let's move the details to a breakout session.'
Check agreement: 'Are we all on the same page?' / 'Do we have a consensus on this?'
Close: 'Let's adjourn here. I'll send the minutes this afternoon, and we'll touch base on Friday.'
answerkey.note: In indirect questions, keep normal word order (subject + verb) and do NOT add 'do/does/did'. Say 'where the report is', not 'where is the report'.

listenings.text: Hi Eliane, this is Robert. I lead project management for a medical-technology company here in Boston, and I run a lot of cross-functional meetings. Let me share how I keep them productive. First, I always send an agenda the day before, so nobody is surprised. During the meeting, I facilitate: I keep us on time, and I make sure the quiet people weigh in, because the best idea often comes from them. If a topic is not ready, I table it and circle back the following week, rather than force a bad decision. At the end, before we adjourn, I check that we have a real consensus and that everyone is on the same page. Then I send the minutes within an hour.

Hi Eliane, it is Karen from the Austin team, following up after this morning's meeting. I just wanted to touch base. Could you let me know whether your clinical team reached a consensus on the training plan? I was also wondering if you could tell me who will facilitate the breakout session next week. There is no rush, but the sooner we are aligned, the sooner we can circle back to the finance team with a clear timeline. I have attached the minutes from today, so we are all on the same page. Feel free to weigh in by email if anything is unclear. Thanks so much, and talk soon.
